name: Behavioral Cohort Analysis to Discover Aha

fields.definition_origin: Methodology pioneered by Facebook growth team (Chamath Palihapitiya, Naomi Gleit, Andy Johns 2008-2012) — comparing behavioral patterns of retained vs churned cohorts to discover the predictive Aha event. Famously codified as Facebook's '7 friends in 10 days' (the behavior most correlated with retained cohort). Generalized in Sean Ellis 'Hacking Growth' (2017) and Reforge growth-engineering curriculum. Slack's '2,000 messages sent in a team' (Cliff Lerner / First Round Review interview with Stewart Butterfield) and Twitter's '30 follows' (Josh Elman) are the other canonical examples. Industry-codified by PostHog, Mixpanel, Amplitude product analytics docs.
fields.core_mechanism: Aha events cannot be reliably guessed by intuition — they must be *discovered* via cohort analysis. Method: define a retention threshold (D7, D30, or paid-conversion); split installs into retained vs churned cohorts; for each candidate behavioral event (sent N messages, added N friends, completed N actions), compute the retention rate of users who hit the event vs users who didn't; the event with the largest retention delta is your candidate Aha. Requires (a) sufficient sample size (typically >1,000 users to detect effect), (b) granular event tracking from Day 0, and (c) statistical discipline to avoid p-hacking. The discovered Aha then becomes the engineering target — design onboarding to drive users toward that behavior. For new apps without enough data, borrow category benchmarks (wellness: first session log; AI-wrapper: first prompt+output; social: first post; fintech: linked bank). The PDF research base specifically calls out PostHog/Mixpanel/Amplitude funnel comparison as the 2026 standard tooling.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: N/A — this is a quantitative-analysis methodology, not a psychological mechanism. Indirect link: the discovered Aha event reflects whatever user behavior most strongly engages reward circuitry, but the methodology itself is statistical.
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Facebook '7 friends in 10 days' — Chamath Palihapitiya / Andy Johns growth-team retro analyses (Stanford talks, podcast interviews 2014+). The threshold was discovered empirically by cohort comparison, not designed a priori. (2) Slack '2,000 messages sent in a team' — Cliff Lerner / First Round Review interview with Stewart Butterfield. Discovered as the team-size + message-density combination most predictive of retained-team status. (3) Twitter '30 follows' — Josh Elman Stanford talk. (4) Dropbox 'one folder shared' — common cited example, public source thinner. (5) Airbnb 'first booking within 7 days' — Brian Chesky public discussions. (6) Sean Ellis 'Hacking Growth' (2017) — formalizes the methodology with multiple case studies. (7) Reforge curriculum (Balfour 2017+) — 600+ SaaS case studies methodology. (8) PostHog / Mixpanel / Amplitude product analytics documentation — provide canonical tooling. (9) Counter-evidence: discovered Aha events can decay over time as the product/users change — Facebook's '7 friends' threshold has reportedly shifted as the network matured. (10) Ethics caveat: cohort analysis can over-fit (correlation, not causation) — randomized A/B tests are required to confirm the discovered event causes retention, not merely correlates.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Retention delta (D7 / D30 retention rate of cohort that hit candidate event vs cohort that didn't), statistical significance of delta (p < 0.05 typical threshold), event-frequency distribution in retained vs churned cohorts, time-to-event distribution, percentage of installs reaching the discovered Aha threshold.
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Discovered Aha events typically yield 2-5x retention delta when comparing reach-event vs not-reach-event cohorts. Facebook's '7 friends in 10 days' had retention delta cited at >3x. Slack's '2,000 messages' had delta around 4-5x. Once discovered and engineered toward, lifts in overall D7 retention range from 10-40% in surveyed apps (Reforge curriculum case studies).
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) Instrument every plausibly-relevant behavioral event from Day 0 (don't wait for hypothesis — capture broadly). Standardize event schema: shared TS types between client and analytics SDK, stable event names. (2) In analytics tool (PostHog/Mixpanel/Amplitude), build a cohort comparison: retained-D30 cohort vs churned-D30 cohort, and for each candidate event, compute the proportion in each cohort plus statistical test. (3) Rank candidate events by retention delta + statistical significance; top 1-3 are Aha candidates. (4) Confirm via randomized A/B test: design an onboarding variant that drives toward candidate Aha, measure D30 retention vs control. (5) For new apps without data: borrow category benchmark (wellness apps: first session log within 24h; AI-wrapper: first prompt+output within 5 min; social: first post within 24h; fintech: linked bank within first session; edtech: first lesson completed within 24h) — set as initial Aha target, iterate post-launch. (6) Cross-cohort segmentation: ad-source / locale / device-class / age-bucket can have different Aha events — segment before generalizing. (7) Re-analyze quarterly — Aha events decay as product matures.
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Facebook '7 friends in 10 days' — discovered via cohort analysis; onboarding retooled to drive friend-discovery Day 0-3. (2) Slack '2,000 messages in a team within first 14 days' — discovered Aha for team-as-customer; onboarding pivoted to seeding team conversations early. (3) Twitter '30 follows' — discovered as the follow-graph density most correlated with active use. (4) Dropbox 'one folder shared' — discovered Aha tied to cross-user sharing, onboarding retooled to prompt sharing. (5) Mixpanel canonical product-analytics dashboard — segment-and-funnel by retained-cohort behavior. (6) PostHog autocapture + cohort comparison pattern — open-source tooling, free tier sufficient for solo dev. (7) Anti-example: a startup that intuited 'first 5 sessions = Aha' without cohort analysis — months of retention work toward wrong target. (8) Cal AI / Mau Baron Prayer Lock: post-launch cohort analysis surely surfaced AI-plan-reveal-completion as the Aha, given the entire architecture's success.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile cohort analysis must account for permission gates (push, ATT, location) which segment cohorts; install attribution (paid vs organic) creates different Aha events per source. Web has cleaner cohort data (no install latency, fewer attribution gaps) but smaller per-user signal density. Mobile-primary for category-borrowed initial Aha (wellness/AI-wrapper apps), but methodology is identical. Mobile-specific events: push opt-in, ATT grant, biometric enrolled — these are themselves candidate Aha-precursors.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: N/A — methodology, not design surface. Indirect alignment: the discovered Aha event then shapes which screens/animations get the peak engineering treatment (item 6 peak-end), and the discovered Aha event drives the goal-gradient (item 7) target.
fields.category_fit: Universal — every category benefits from cohort-driven Aha discovery. Critical for: any app with >10K installs (where statistical power is sufficient), any app where intuition has been wrong (most). Less applicable: pre-launch apps (no data — borrow benchmarks instead), pure-utility (calculator has no meaningful Aha event beyond first use).
fields.tooling_options: PostHog (free tier 1M events/mo — sufficient for solo dev; cohort comparison + funnels + retention reports built in), Mixpanel (free up to 100K monthly tracked users — more polished dashboards), Amplitude (free up to 50K MTU + paid plans), Heap (autocapture-first, lower instrumentation cost), Hightouch (reverse-ETL to push cohorts back to product). For event schema management: Avo, Iteratively. For visualization: any of the above ship dashboards out of the box. Recommended for solo dev: PostHog (open-source, generous free tier, includes session recording + feature flags + experiments).
fields.ai_dependency: none: cohort analysis is statistical methodology + product analytics SDK; no LLM call required. Optional: AI-assisted event-name generation (Avo + Claude), AI-assisted hypothesis generation from raw event logs (early-stage research, not production-ready in 2026).
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude to instrument events broadly and define a cohort-discovery dashboard. Example fragment 1: 'Instrument 30+ candidate behavioral events from Day 0 (granular: per-screen-viewed, per-action-completed, per-feature-used, per-permission-granted). Use a shared TS schema (PropertyDefinitions interface). Send to PostHog with a stable event name and rich properties (step_id, locale, ad_variant, device_class). Build a Retention Cohorts dashboard in PostHog showing D7/D30 retention conditional on each candidate event. Identify the top 3 events with largest retention delta + p<0.05 — those are Aha candidates.' Example fragment 2: 'For new app pre-launch: set initial Aha target based on category benchmark (wellness: first session log within 24h). Instrument the event, define an onboarding variant that drives toward it, ship to a cohort, measure D7 retention vs control. Iterate per release.'
fields.stitch_application: N/A — Stitch is a visual mockup tool, not analytics tooling. Indirect: design dashboards in Stitch that visualize the cohort comparison (e.g., for an internal dashboard product) — but the methodology itself is non-visual.
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen defaults to: (1) instrumenting only 'onboarding_completed' and 'paywall_converted' (no behavioral granularity for cohort discovery); (2) generic event names without rich properties (untrackable to ad-source / locale / variant); (3) no shared TS schema (events drift between client and dashboard); (4) suggesting an Aha event by intuition rather than analysis. Counter-prompt: 'Instrument 30+ candidate behavioral events from Day 0 using a shared TS schema. Required properties on every event: user_id, session_id, step_id (if applicable), locale, ad_variant, device_class. Build a PostHog cohort-comparison dashboard for D7 retention conditional on each event. Forbid suggesting an Aha event without cohort data — for pre-launch apps, use category benchmarks and label as \'borrowed\', not \'discovered\'. Output the candidate-events list with rationale for each.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Intuiting Aha without cohort analysis (most common error in solo-dev apps). (2) P-hacking — running 100 cohort comparisons and reporting the one with p<0.05 without correction. (3) Confusing correlation with causation — discovered Aha event MIGHT just correlate with retention (e.g., users who engage broadly hit many events; the 'Aha' is downstream of the real cause). (4) Over-fitting on small samples (<1,000 users) — false positives common. (5) Generalizing across cohorts when ad-source / locale / age-bucket have different Aha events. (6) Setting Aha event then never re-analyzing as product/users mature. (7) Borrowing category benchmark and treating it as discovered (label as borrowed, not discovered, until validated). (8) Instrumenting only conversion events (paywall_converted) without behavioral granularity (kills cohort-discovery capability).
fields.regulatory_risk: Low-Medium. (1) Behavioral event tracking is subject to GDPR/CCPA/CMP requirements — must be declared in privacy policy, with appropriate consent mechanisms. (2) Apple ATT requires user permission for behavioral tracking across apps; analytics within your own app are usually exempt but still must be declared. (3) Apple Guideline 5.1.1(v) data minimization — ensure tracked events tie to declared core function. (4) COPPA (2026-04-22 deadline) — under-13 cohort analysis requires verifiable parental consent on extended tracking. (5) GDPR-K — under-16 EU cohort analysis requires explicit consent. (6) Cohort analysis itself is benign; the data-collection that powers it is regulated.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple ATT framework constrains cross-app tracking (within-app analytics typically OK). Apple Privacy Manifest required (PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy) declaring analytics SDK and tracking purposes. Google Play Data Safety section requires declaration of behavioral profiling. PostHog / Mixpanel / Amplitude all provide compliance tooling for GDPR/CCPA/COPPA — verify SDK version is current.
fields.accessibility_implications: N/A — methodology has no direct accessibility implications. Indirect: ensure users with assistive tech (VoiceOver/TalkBack) generate cohort data — events should fire from accessibility-driven actions (not just touch). Test event firing with VoiceOver to ensure no behavioral data gap for users with disabilities.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Sample-size minimum: <1,000 cohort users yields unreliable Aha discovery; below 10K users, treat findings as directional. (2) Time-to-event timing matters — '7 friends' is meaningless without 'in 10 days'; the time window must be specified. (3) Correlation-vs-causation — confirm via randomized A/B test before betting onboarding architecture on a discovered Aha. (4) Cohort drift: Aha events decay as product changes; re-analyze quarterly. (5) Survivorship bias — retained cohort had more sessions to fire events; control for session-count when comparing. (6) Multiple-comparisons problem — running many cohort splits inflates false-positive rate; use Bonferroni correction or False Discovery Rate. (7) Pre-launch bootstrap: borrow category benchmark, label as borrowed not discovered, validate post-launch. (8) Ethics: behavioral tracking that powers cohort analysis must comply with consent regimes and avoid surveilling kids without VPC.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Methodology is universal. Discovered Aha events vary cross-culturally: Facebook's '7 friends in 10 days' is a Western-derived threshold; LATAM users may have higher friend-graph density (different threshold), JP users have lower public-network density (different signal entirely — group/clan messages may matter more than friend count). Locale-segmented cohort analysis is critical when shipping to diverse markets. RTL has no methodology impact but ensures event names / dashboards are not LTR-biased.
fields.prescribed_action: Instrument 30+ candidate behavioral events from Day 0 with a shared TS schema, build a PostHog cohort-comparison dashboard for D7/D30 retention conditional on each event, and identify the top 1-3 Aha candidates. For pre-launch apps, borrow category benchmarks (wellness: first session log; AI-wrapper: first prompt+output; social: first post; fintech: linked bank) labeled as borrowed-not-discovered, then validate post-launch via cohort analysis.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: Questionnaire steps are themselves cohort-discovery surfaces — instrument per-step viewed/completed/abandoned with locale + ad-source properties, then segment cohorts by which step caused drop-off. The Aha candidate (likely AI plan reveal completion) shapes the questionnaire's narrative arc.
fields.aha_relevance: This item IS the methodology for *discovering* Aha. Item 9 distinguishes Aha from Activation conceptually; this item provides the empirical method for finding the specific Aha event in your app. Together they form the Aha-discovery + Aha-engineering pipeline.
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: N/A — methodology, not design surface. Indirectly: the discovered Aha event shapes which archetype voice is amplified at the peak (item 52 archetype-driven onboarding voice).
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Intuited Aha (no cohort data); No Telemetry (can't discover what you don't measure); Vanity Metric (DAU/MAU) (masks quality vs LTV cohort signal); P-Hacking; Single-Source Generalization (assuming US Aha = JP Aha).
sources: [First Round Review — Stewart Butterfield Slack Interview (2,000 messages)](https://review.firstround.com/From-0-to-1B-Slacks-Founder-Shares-Their-Epic-Launch-Strategy)
[Andy Johns / Josh Elman — Stanford Growth Talks (Facebook 7 friends, Twitter 30 follows)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lrwRtb0VjZ4)
